<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567262 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04693: Basic concepts

- page_id: `249567262`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567262/Basic+concepts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:26:53.134+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Entity-relationship (ER) modeling and diagrams
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An entity is a thing capable of an existence. An entity usually refers to some aspect of the real world, which can be distinguished from other aspects of the real world (a person, place, customer transaction, order, etc.).

An entity is represented by a box shape on the diagram. An entity has two compartments where properties (columns) of the entity can be specified. The upper compartment holds the primary key properties of the entity; the lower compartment holds other properties.

A relationship between entities describes how entities are associated. Relationships are represented by lines, connecting entities. Relationship end adornments indicate multiplicities of these ends. Multiplicity is the number of entity instances that can be associated with a number of another entity instances. Relationship multiplicity is represented by three symbols, the so-called “crow's foot notation” or “Information Engineering notation”.

The following table shows symbols of the relationship multiplicity.

| Name | Value | Notation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Zero | Zero |  |
| Vertical | One |  |
| Crow's foot | Many |  |

Multiplicity lower bounds and upper bounds are paired into one adornment, as shown in the following table. Note that any lower bound, which is more that 0 is treated as 1 (this also includes lower bounds greater than 1, such as 2.). Also, any upper bound which is greater than 1 is treated as Many (this also includes upper bounds less than unlimited, such as 7.).

| Min | Max | Read as | Figure |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0 | 1 | One (optional) |  |
| 1 | 1 | One (mandatory) |  |
| 0 | Many | Many (optional) |  |
| 1 | Many | Many (mandatory) |  |

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Basic ER diagram example.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- Some authors use Entity Type term to signify the description of a set of entities and Entity Instance term to signify concrete exemplar from that set. Entity term used in this manual corresponds to Entity Type .
- Data modeling world frequently uses term Cardinality to denote the allowable numbers of entity instances, which can be associated. But with the rise of the UML, the more correct term Multiplicity was introduced and term Cardinality is only used to denote concrete numbers of entity instances, which are associated. Hence in the example Person [0..1]------
- -[0..*] Book, the ranges [0..1] and [0..*] are called multiplicities. And if we have person “John Doe” associated with books “Moby Dick” and “Origin of Species”, we have a cardinality of 2 for loaned books role (and 1 on an opposite end - current reader role). Note that cardinality is always concrete number while multiplicity denotes range of possible cardinalities.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An entity is a thing capable of an existence. An entity usually refers to some aspect of the real world, which can be distinguished from other aspects of the real world (a person, place, customer transaction, order, etc.).</p><p>An entity is represented by a box shape on the diagram. An entity has two compartments where properties (columns) of the entity can be specified. The upper compartment holds the primary key properties of the entity; the lower compartment holds other properties.</p><p>A relationship between entities describes how entities are associated. Relationships are represented by lines, connecting entities. Relationship end adornments indicate multiplicities of these ends. Multiplicity is the number of entity instances that can be associated with a number of another entity instances. Relationship multiplicity is represented by three symbols, the so-called “crow's foot notation” or “Information Engineering notation”.</p><p>The following table shows symbols of the relationship multiplicity.</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 69.9945%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 42.9451%;" /><col style="width: 24.9216%;" /><col style="width: 32.1333%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: left;">Name</th><th style="text-align: left;">Value</th><th style="text-align: left;">Notation</th></tr><tr><td>Zero</td><td>Zero</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Zero Notation.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td>Vertical</td><td>One</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Vertical Notation.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td><p>Crow's foot</p></td><td>Many</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Crow's Foot Notation.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr></tbody></table><p>Multiplicity lower bounds and upper bounds are paired into one adornment, as shown in the following table. Note that any lower bound, which is more that 0 is treated as 1 (this also includes lower bounds greater than 1, such as 2.). Also, any upper bound which is greater than 1 is treated as Many (this also includes upper bounds less than unlimited, such as 7.).</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 70.1042%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 17.7617%;" /><col style="width: 25.3511%;" /><col style="width: 24.7285%;" /><col style="width: 32.1587%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Min</th><th>Max</th><th>Read as</th><th>Figure</th></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>1</td><td>One (optional)</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="One Optional Pairing.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td>1</td><td>1</td><td>One (mandatory)</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="One Mandatory Pairing.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>Many</td><td>Many (optional)</td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many Optional Pairing.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">1</td><td colspan="1">Many</td><td colspan="1">Many (mandatory)</td><td colspan="1"><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Many Mandatory Pairing.png" /></ac:image></p></div></td></tr></tbody></table><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Basic ER Diagram Example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Basic ER diagram example.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b767ba70-1442-4a55-8ae0-36e002775508"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Some authors use <strong>Entity Type </strong>term to signify the description of a set of entities and <strong>Entity </strong><strong>Instance </strong>term to signify concrete exemplar from that set. <strong>Entity </strong>term used in this manual corresponds to <strong>Entity Type</strong>.</li><li>Data modeling world frequently uses term Cardinality to denote the allowable numbers of entity instances, which can be associated. But with the rise of the UML, the more correct term <strong>Multiplicity </strong>was introduced and term <strong>Cardinality </strong>is only used to denote concrete numbers of entity instances, which are associated. Hence in the example Person [0..1]------</li><li>-[0..*] Book, the ranges [0..1] and [0..*] are called multiplicities. And if we have person “John Doe” associated with books “Moby Dick” and “Origin of Species”, we have a cardinality of 2 for loaned books role (and 1 on an opposite end - current reader role). Note that cardinality is always concrete number while multiplicity denotes range of possible cardinalities.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249005799 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04694: Basic requirement concepts

- page_id: `249005799`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249005799/Basic+requirement+concepts
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-17T23:11:27.405+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Requirements management
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section contains all basic concepts that are used while using the Cameo Requirements Modeler Plugin.

Basic requirement concepts:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966784 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04696: Basic tasks in tables

- page_id: `243966784`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966784/Basic+tasks+in+tables
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:09.110+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > Tables
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Important

218215203

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Important

218215208

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Important

218215207

Tables are diagrams which allow you to manage any amount of model data in a spreadsheet-like form. It is much easier to analyze a group of data in one place, especially when you can create new elements, add existing elements, edit their properties directly from the table, and paste data from the clipboard. This allows you to sort and filter data, show, hide, reorder columns, and clear or edit individual cells. Exporting tables into more commonly used file formats, such as *.html*, *.csv*, or *.xlsx*, makes it simple to share them with stakeholders.

#### NOTE: Important

Important

Each table row represents an element. Each column represents element properties. The column header and row number header are highlighted in grey when selecting single or multiple cells.

The following procedures provide the basic tasks to get you started working with all modeling tool tables:

218215198

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Legends' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561507 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04697: Behavior to structure synchronization

- page_id: `249561507`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561507/Behavior+to+structure+synchronization
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:13:06.522+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1084440424

1084440437

1084440427

The behavior to structure synchronization checks the consistency of the Internal Block diagram (IBD) with Activities, automatically detects inconsistencies and enables users to handle each inconsistency individually by providing solvers. As a result, the coherence of IBDs with Activities is ensured at any stage of the model development process.

The behavior to structure synchronization validates the Part Properties displayed in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space=''] to check for compatible

- Proxy Ports
- Flow Properties
- Interface Blocks
- Connectors
- Item Flows

To verify whether or not the structural elements (e.g., a Proxy Port and an Interface Block) and behavioral elements (e.g., a flow) are compatible, validation rules are used. If any inconsistencies are found, they are highlighted in yellow in the Internal Block Diagram and displayed in the**Validation Results** panel. Then, they can be individually handled using solvers.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Synchronizing behavior to structure.

##### Checking behavior to structure synchronization

You need to set the **Allocation Mode**(**Options**>**Project**>**SysML**>**Allocation Mode**)project optionto **Usage** before you can synchronize behavior to structure.

To check the structure model consistency with the behavior model

1. In the toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram, click [ATTACHMENT filename='tools_button.png'] Tools.
2. In the open drop-down menu, select Behavior to Structure Synchronization. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The behavior to structure synchronization check is initiated.

484219123

**Continue reading**

Then, they can be individually handled using solvers.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="structure_and_behavior_models_synchronization.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Synchronizing behavior to structure.</h6><h3>Checking behavior to structure synchronization </h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7e7c0f2-00c7-404d-a569-4855cca3bc1b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You need to set the <strong>Allocation Mode</strong><span> </span><span>(</span><strong>Options</strong><span> &gt;</span><strong> Project</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>SysML</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Allocation Mode</strong><span>) </span>project option<span> </span>to <strong>Usage</strong> before you can synchronize behavior to structure.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />To check the structure model consistency with the behavior model</p><hr /><ol><li>In the toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tools_button.png" /></ac:image>Tools.</li><li>In the open drop-down menu, select <strong>Behavior to Structure Synchronization.</strong><br /><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="behavior_to_structure_sync_button.png" /></ac:image><br /></strong></li></ol><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a4344bbf-9a03-49f3-8337-2857606c756f">The behavior to structure synchronization check is initiated.</ac:inline-comment-marker> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b67cc1f-75c3-42fa-b535-cd642921b424"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">484219123</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Continue reading</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="3cb859a5-2ed8-42c2-b553-a77c7e0bf462" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561895 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04698: Blackbox ICD Table

- page_id: `249561895`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561895/Blackbox+ICD+Table
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:13:12.075+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > SysML v1 diagram descriptions
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Blackbox ICD Table represents all external Ports/interfaces of the selected Block. The table is useful when trying to identify what kind of inputs and outputs are available in the selected system.

In the following example, the distiller system has two inputs (*dirty water*and *q in*) and three outputs (*bypass*, *purified* and *sludge*). In this case, the Port *dirty water*, Port *purified*, Port *bypass* support such value properties as: *water temp*, *water press*, etc. Pay attention, that the input to distiller is a *dirty water* (row 1) and the output from distiller is *purified*water (row 4). Both of them contains the same properties but it may have different values defined (they are not defined in the figure). [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Interface Control Document tables' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Blackbox ICD Table of the Distiller Block.

A Blackbox ICD Table consists of the following columns.

| Column name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| # | A row number. |
| ~ ^ | Displays if Port is conjugated (~) or inherited (^). |
| Port Name | A Port name. |
| Port Type | A Port type. |
| Type Features | Displays all the features (e.g., Flow property, Part property) of the Port type. |
| Direction | Displays a combined direction prefix of the Port. |
| Documentation | Displays a documentation text. If it is not described, click to edit. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562276 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04699: Block structure

- page_id: `249562276`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562276/Block+structure
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:13:16.284+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model > Blocks and Parts
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The three components are connected through ports that correspond electrical pins. The source and the resistor each have both a positive and a negative pin, whereas the ground has only one positive pin. Electrical energy (carried by electric charge) is transmitted between components through the pins. The image below contains the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] that illustrates this.

A new abstract block *TwoPinComponent* is created to illustrate abstract components that have two ports (pins). These two ports are named *p*******and*n*, and they are both of the *ChargeFlowElement*type. ChargeFlowElement belongs to the simulation library (*SysPhSLibrary.mdzip*), and it can be selected by unchecking **Apply Filter**in the type list.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *p* and*n* ports typed by *ChargeFlow Element*Interface Block.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The three components are connected through ports that correspond electrical pins. The source and the resistor each have both a positive and a negative pin, whereas the ground has only one positive pin. Electrical energy (carried by electric charge) is transmitted between components through the pins. The image below contains the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> that illustrates this. </p><p>A new abstract block <em>TwoPinComponent</em> is created to illustrate abstract components that have two ports (pins). These two ports are named <em>p</em><strong><em> </em> </strong>and<em> n</em>, and they are both of the <em>ChargeFlowElement </em>type. ChargeFlowElement belongs to the simulation library (<em>SysPhSLibrary.mdzip</em>), and it can be selected by unchecking <strong>Apply Filter  </strong>in the type list. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="470"><ri:attachment ri:filename="electrical_components.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The <em>p</em> and<em> n</em> ports typed by <em>ChargeFlow Element </em>Interface Block.</span></h6><p><br /></p><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249562270 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04700: Blocks and Parts

- page_id: `249562270`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249562270/Blocks+and+Parts
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:13:15.879+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Exporting to External Simulation Models > Sample models > Creating a simple model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1553744160

1553744160

1553744160

The electrical circuit model has the main *block* and the*circuit*. To model the structure of the circuit, create a Circuit [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='MED'] and an associated [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']. The circuit has three parts: *a source*, *a ground*, and *a resistor*. These parts are of a different type since they all have a different behavior. It is necessary to create a Block for each of these parts. The picture below shows how the Internal Block Diagram look like.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The *Circuit* IBD consists of three parts: *source*, *ground* and *resistor*.

1553744159

**Related pages:**

****

To model the structure of the circuit, create a Circuit <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Block" /></ac:link> and an associated <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Internal Block Diagram (IBD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The circuit has three parts: <em>a source</em>, <em>a ground</em>, and <em>a resistor</em>. These parts are of a different type since they all have a different behavior. It is necessary to create a Block for each of these parts. The picture below shows how the Internal Block Diagram look like. </p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ibd_sample_model.png" /></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">The <em>Circuit</em> IBD consists of three parts: <em>source</em>, <em>ground</em> and <em>resistor</em>.</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a28dfc3c-f9c4-4c32-b5d8-8f5dd784e13d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1553744159</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages:</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b30fcee2-0b0c-40d0-92aa-37f61062237b" /><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 04701: Bookmark tool

- page_id: `243970805`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243970805/Bookmark+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:58.876+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Helper tools
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

2050367121

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

2050367135

2050367115

**On this page**

43

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

2050367125

$bookmark contains utility functions for bookmarking. The functions of this module are accessible from templates through **$bookmark**.

#### INFO: Information

Information

The default style of bookmarks depends on the editor used. For example, Microsoft Word 2003 displays hyperlinks in blue.

****

###### **$bookmark.openURL(url, content)**

Create a hyperlink to open a URL.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | url | java.lang.String | A URL text. |
| content | java.lang.Object | The text content. |  |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Link | The text content with a hyperlink. |

For example:

```text

```

****

###### **$bookmark.openURL(url, content)**

Create a hyperlink to open a URL.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | url | java.net.URL | A URL instance. |
| content | java.lang.Object | The text content. |  |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Link | The text content with a hyperlink. |

For example:

```text

```

****

###### **$bookmark.openURL(uri, content)**

Create a hyperlink to open a URI.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | uri | java.net.URI | A URI instance. |
| content | java.lang.Object | The text content. |  |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Link | The content with a hyperlink. |

For example:

```text

```

****

###### **$bookmark.open(content)**

Create a hyperlink for a bookmark. The bookmark ID will be automatically generated from the content.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | content | java.lang.Object | The text content. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Link | The text content with a hyperlink. |

****

###### **$bookmark.open(bookmarkId, content)**

Create a hyperlink for a bookmark. The bookmark ID value must match the parameter bookmark ID that passes onto the link.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | bookmarkId | java.lang.String | The bookmark ID. |
|  | content | java.lang.Object | The text content. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Link | The text content with a hyperlink. |

For example:

```text

```

###### **$bookmark.create(bookmarkObject)**

Create a bookmark. The bookmark ID will be automatically generated from the bookmark object.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | bookmarkObject | java.lang.Object | A bookmark object or content. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Bookmark | The content with a bookmark. |

****

###### **$bookmark.create(bookmarkId, bookmarkObject)**

Create a bookmark. The default element type for this function is "label".

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | bookmarkId | java.lang.String | The bookmark ID. |
|  | bookmarkObject | java.lang.Object | A bookmark object or content. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Bookmark | The content with a bookmark. |

For example:

```text

```

###### **$bookmark.create(bookmarkId, bookmarkObject, elementType)**

Create a bookmark by specifying an element type.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | bookmarkId | java.lang.String | The bookmark ID. |
|  | bookmarkObject | java.lang.Object | A bookmark object or content. |
|  | elementType | java.lang.String | An element type name, e.g., html tag. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Bookmark | The content with a bookmark. |

****

###### **$bookmark.getBookmarkId(id)**

Return a bookmark ID from a given string.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | id | java.lang.String | An original string value. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.Bookmark | The bookmark ID. |

****

###### **$bookmark.createRef(referenceID)**

Create a reference bookmark of any Microsoft Word reference numbered items.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | referenceID | java.lang.String | A reference ID. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.TextReference | The content with a bookmark in the Microsoft Word format. |

****

###### **$bookmark.openRef(referenceID)**

Create a hyperlink for a reference bookmark of any Microsoft Word reference numbered items.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | referenceID | java.lang.String | A reference ID. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.TextReference | The content with a bookmark in the Microsoft Word format. |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can press F9 to replace the text content shown as **Refer** with the updated numbered item that it refers to.

****

###### **$bookmark.openRef(referenceID, referenceText)**

Create a hyperlink for a reference bookmark of any Microsoft Word reference numbered items.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | referenceID | java.lang.String | A reference ID. |
|  | referenceText | java.lang.String | The text content. |
| Return | - | com.nomagic.magicreport.TextReference | The content with a bookmark in the Microsoft Word format. |

#### NOTE: Note

Note

You can press F9 to replace the text content shown as **Refer referenceText** with the updated numbered item that it refers to.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566624 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 04702: Boundary Events

- page_id: `249566624`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566624/Boundary+Events
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:58:31.239+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > BPMN Process Diagram
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Boundary Event is an Intermediate event which can be placed on the boundary of any of the following activities:

- SubProcess, Task, or Call Activity
- SubChoreography, Choreography Task, or Call Choreography

Boundary Event indicates that while attached-to Activity is running, event is listening for the trigger signal.

Boundary Event types:

- Interrupting Boundary Event aborts Activity and Process is continued of exceptional flow. This event is drawn with a solid border.
- Non-Interrupting Boundary Event splits process to parallel flows. This event is drawn with a dashed border.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Error Boundary Event attached to SubProcess

Types of Boundary Events are as follows:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Boundary Event is an Intermediate event which can be placed on the boundary of any of the following activities:</p><ul><li>SubProcess, Task, or Call Activity</li><li>SubChoreography, Choreography Task, or Call Choreography</li></ul><p>Boundary Event indicates that while attached-to Activity is running, event is listening for the trigger signal.</p><p>Boundary Event types:</p><ul><li>Interrupting Boundary Event aborts Activity and Process is continued of exceptional flow. This event is drawn with a solid border.</li><li>Non-Interrupting Boundary Event splits process to parallel flows. This event is drawn with a dashed border.</li></ul><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="boundary_error.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Error Boundary Event attached to SubProcess</h6><p>Types of Boundary Events are as follows:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="dd5528d2-89f7-4470-8751-3f740afa86c7" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566975 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 04703: BPMN Activities Description Table

- page_id: `249566975`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566975/BPMN+Activities+Description+Table
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:43:58.268+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > BPMN Tables
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A BPMN Activities Description table is designed to display or edit selected process activities descriptions in a table form. A BPMN Activities Description table is created for a BPMN Process.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Activities Description table

**Related elements**

- Activities
- BPMN Tables

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>A BPMN Activities Description table is designed to display or edit selected process activities descriptions in a table form. A BPMN Activities Description table is created for a BPMN Process.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="activities_description_table.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Activities Description table</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="695c5257-0aee-4e84-a638-bd72cf762daa"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Activities">Activities</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link" data-uuid="9db0906e-3fdb-4e9a-acfc-a3f0fadcc332"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Tables">BPMN Tables</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="31b07efe-57fa-4701-aaad-95395c00aa19"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566915 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04704: BPMN Association

- page_id: `249566915`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566915/BPMN+Association
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:22:22.782+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > Business Data Diagram
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1487823057

1487823057

1487823057

**Description**

A BPMN Association link shows that particular classes are related to each other. BPMN Association links may have names defined – names help to read diagrams. You can also specify multiplicity at the end of a BPMN Association.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Association between two Classes with Multiplicity at the End

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566772 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04706: BPMN Choreography Diagram

- page_id: `249566772`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566772/BPMN+Choreography+Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:22:15.715+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Business Process Model and Notation Concepts
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1486494018

1486494019

1486494019

**Description**

A Choreography formalizes the way business participants coordinate their interactions. A Choreography is a type of process, but its purpose and behavior are different from a standard BPMN process.

A standard process defines the flow of activities of a specific partner entity or organization. In contrast, a Choreography formalizes the way business participants coordinate their interactions. The focus is not on the work performed within these participants, but rather on the information (messages) exchanged between them.

A Choreography is a definition of expected behavior, basically a procedural business contract between interacting participants. It shows the messages exchanged and their logical relations. This allows business partners to plan their business processes for inter-operation without introducing conflicts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Choreography diagram

A BPMN Choreography diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram and includes restrictions and extensions as defined by BPMN.

Types of Choreography elements are as follows:

1486494017

**Related elements**

- Choreography
- Choreography Activities
- Error
- Intermediate Catch Event
- Intermediate Throwing Event
- Boundary Events
- End Events
- Gateways

**Related diagram**

- BPMN Process Diagram

**Related procedure**

- Using BPMN Choreography Diagram

The focus is not on the work performed within these participants, but rather on the information (messages) exchanged between them.</p><p>A Choreography is a definition of expected behavior, basically a procedural business contract between interacting participants. It shows the messages exchanged and their logical relations. This allows business partners to plan their business processes for inter-operation without introducing conflicts.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="choreography_diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Choreography diagram</h6><p>A BPMN Choreography diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram and includes restrictions and extensions as defined by BPMN.</p><p>Types of Choreography elements are as follows:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="4d335140-5180-4a64-b441-3516fbfd43f8" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="726b7a0e-3517-4fcc-aa78-06ee22b04212"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1486494017</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Choreography">Choreography</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Choreography+Activities">Choreography Activities</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Error">Error</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Intermediate+Catch+Event">Intermediate Catch Event</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Intermediate+Throwing+Event">Intermediate Throwing Event</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Boundary+Events">Boundary Events</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/End+Events">End Events</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Gateways">Gateways</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagram</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Using+BPMN+Choreography+Diagram">Using BPMN Choreography Diagram</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566736 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04707: BPMN Collaboration Diagram

- page_id: `249566736`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566736/BPMN+Collaboration+Diagram
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:22:14.059+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Business Process Model and Notation Concepts
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1486122303

1486122304

1486122304

**Description**

A Collaboration represents the interactions between two or more business entities. A Collaboration diagram depicts a global point of view. It shows the interactions between participants in general.

A Collaboration contains two or more pools, representing the participants in the collaboration. Messages exchanged between the participants are shown by message flows that connect two pools together (or objects within the pools).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Collaboration diagram with two Black Box Pools

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Collaboration diagram with two Pools showing process

A Collaboration diagram can also show distinct conversations between collaborating participants in a domain. Communications are defined by the conversations, participants, and conversation links between them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Collaboration diagram with Conversations

The elements of a BPMN Process diagram can be displayed on the BPMN Collaboration diagram.

1486122303

**Related element**

- BPMN Process

**Related diagram**

- BPMN Process Diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Messages exchanged between the participants are shown by message flows that connect two pools together (or objects within the pools).</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="collaboration_I.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Collaboration diagram with two Black Box Pools</h6><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="collaboration_II.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Collaboration diagram with two Pools showing process</h6><p>A Collaboration diagram can also show distinct conversations between collaborating participants in a domain. Communications are defined by the conversations, participants, and conversation links between them.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="collaboration_III.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Collaboration diagram with Conversations</h6><p>The elements of a BPMN Process diagram can be displayed on the BPMN Collaboration diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2bcef008-ec4c-4195-84a8-a37d5e81a3b4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1486122303</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related element</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process">BPMN Process</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagram</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 04708: BPMN Data Usage Matrices

- page_id: `249566983`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566983/BPMN+Data+Usage+Matrices
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:39:47.585+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > BPMN Matrices
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A BPMN Data Usage Matrix shows what data classes are used in BPMN Processes and SubProcess. Data Objects represents classes in a BPMN diagram.

A BPMN Data Usage Matrix presents information about all BPMN Processes and SubProcesses in rows and Classes in columns. Each row in the matrix shows if a class is used in a BPMN Process or SubProcess diagram as a Data Object.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Data Usage Matrix

**Related elements**

- BPMN Process
- SubProcesses
- Class
- BPMN Matrices

**Related diagram**

- BPMN Process Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- Dependency Matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Data Objects represents classes in a BPMN diagram.</p><p>A BPMN Data Usage Matrix presents information about all BPMN Processes and SubProcesses in rows and Classes in columns. Each row in the matrix shows if a class is used in a BPMN Process or SubProcess diagram as a Data Object.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="data_usage_matrix.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Data Usage Matrix</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="8a678172-4e84-4051-93e8-3ad7905acea2"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process">BPMN Process</a></li><li data-uuid="3b1e47f1-ae45-4def-8135-3ec2cc6c6c12"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/SubProcesses">SubProcesses</a></li><li data-uuid="adb9546d-6105-42b1-9925-dcc64e6b07fe"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Class">Class</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link" data-uuid="64d5c999-ac19-4c28-9ccc-9b493db1eca3"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Matrices">BPMN Matrices</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagram</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="1c264b88-0dcb-4684-b635-9527890d142d"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="076434e6-dc42-4c53-9a5c-4af2e2c40594"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="79c6266a-9c9c-44a4-9d26-878d3510dc3a"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Dependency+Matrix">Dependency Matrix</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243958974 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 04710: BPMN modeling

- page_id: `243958974`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243958974/BPMN+modeling
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:54:20.967+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Cameo Business Modeler Plugin provides implementation of the Business Process Modeling and Notation (BPMN) standard and the best capability for understanding internal business procedures. The notation includes performance, collaboration, and business transactions in your business between organizations. For service providers and consultants, Cameo Business Modeler Plugin is also the ideal communication tool. Not only do you get the benefits of a standard business notation, but also many others that you and your customer/stakeholder can trust and understand. With collaboration and reporting tools, you can integrate your customer right into your process.

To learn more, see the following topics:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p style="text-align: left;">The Cameo Business Modeler Plugin provides implementation of the Business Process Modeling and Notation (BPMN) standard and the best capability for understanding internal business procedures. The notation includes performance, collaboration, and business transactions in your business between organizations. For service providers and consultants, Cameo Business Modeler Plugin is also the ideal communication tool. Not only do you get the benefits of a standard business notation, but also many others that you and your customer/stakeholder can trust and understand. With collaboration and reporting tools, you can integrate your customer right into your process.</p><p style="text-align: left;">To learn more, see the following topics:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d054600b-d5b5-4386-b9be-1f7c6f0d6366" /></p>
````

1485168492

1485168493

1485168493

**Description**

A BPMN Process element defines a process performed in an organization. This element is a container for the BPMN Process diagram and its elements. Process diagram describes how a process is performed.

**Notation** 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1485168491

**Related elements**

- Tasks
- Process Definition Diagram

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566469 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 04712: BPMN Process Diagram

- page_id: `249566469`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566469/BPMN+Process+Diagram
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:45:09.173+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Business Process Model and Notation Concepts
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A BPMN Process Diagram describes a sequence or flow of activities in an organization that shows how the business works. The diagram shows activities, events, and data that trigger or feed business activities. A BPMN Process Diagram is similar to the UML Activity diagram with a much richer set of default message types and business process styles of notations.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Process diagram

**Related elements**

- BPMN Process
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>A BPMN Process Diagram describes a sequence or flow of activities in an organization that shows how the business works. The diagram shows activities, events, and data that trigger or feed business activities. A BPMN Process Diagram is similar to the UML Activity diagram with a much richer set of default message types and business process styles of notations.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="process_diagram.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">BPMN Process diagram</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="cc08b33f-4bd0-477c-8df8-9cd086810848"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process">BPMN Process</a></li><li data-uuid="821cbae5-3519-47f0-bbb3-7d94a4a2051c"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566986 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 04714: BPMN Processes Structure Map

- page_id: `249566986`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566986/BPMN+Processes+Structure+Map
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:22:27.978+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

148567171

148567172

148567172

**Description**

A BPMN Processes Structure Map diagram allows you to represent the structure of processes in a project. The 
processes are grouped into packages on the diagram. And each process may itself contains other processes, 
which in turn may contain other processes. The diagram makes the processes easier to understand and helps you 
analyze how they are organized.

A BPMN Processes Structure Map diagram shows packages, processes, subprocesses, and tasks from a 
selected scope.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Processes Structure Map

148567170

**Related elements**

- Package
- BPMN Process
- Activities

**Related diagram**

- BPMN Process Diagram

**Related external resource**

- Relation Map

The diagram makes the processes easier to understand and helps you<br />analyze how they are organized.</p><p>A BPMN Processes Structure Map diagram shows packages, processes, subprocesses, and tasks from a<br />selected scope.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="process_structure_map.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Processes Structure Map</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ff4ed806-386e-41d5-9504-3159473e5ea8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">148567170</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Package">Package</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process">BPMN Process</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Activities">Activities</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagram</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related external resource</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Relation+Map">Relation Map</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566969 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 04716: BPMN Resources Description Table

- page_id: `249566969`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566969/BPMN+Resources+Description+Table
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:41:41.248+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > BPMN Tables
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A BPMN Resources Description table is designed to display or edit resources description in a table form. You can display existing Resources or create new ones in this table.

BPMN defines four types of Resources as follows:

- Resource
- Organization Unit
- Role
- Person
- Information System

You can display all of them in a BPMN Resources Description table.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Resource Roles Description table

**Related elements**

- Resource
- BPMN Tables

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>A BPMN Resources Description table is designed to display or edit resources description in a table form. You can display existing Resources or create new ones in this table.</p><p>BPMN defines four types of Resources as follows:</p><ul><li data-uuid="11ed7d5d-dde7-4511-b77e-4a37222ffdad">Resource</li><li data-uuid="cbadfedd-55c5-4b68-8784-8e6e809782fd">Organization Unit</li><li data-uuid="85db651e-8974-46d5-8528-ad27739b323b">Role</li><li data-uuid="e2aff2e1-d9f6-4e6d-90e6-8596a965c5a3">Person</li><li data-uuid="d1465541-b301-4bd1-8f50-c8ec47954ddc">Information System</li></ul><p>You can display all of them in a BPMN Resources Description table.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_description_table.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>BPMN Resource Roles Description table</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="97cfb87c-bccb-4781-939e-057e40d7afcd"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Resource">Resource</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link" data-uuid="cbe23566-5b8d-45a7-a19c-aeff59a1f6b6"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/BPMN+Tables">BPMN Tables</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="db17430e-d860-40c0-9238-14a9e6030ea7"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating a Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249566980 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 04717: BPMN Resources Usage Matrices

- page_id: `249566980`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249566980/BPMN+Resources+Usage+Matrices
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-10T13:39:00.943+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > BPMN Matrices
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A BPMN Resources Usage Matrix is designed to review and edit an allocation of Resource Roles for BPMN Activities. The Resource Roles used in an Activity show a Resources property in a BPMN Activity specification dialog.

A BPMN Resources Usage Matrix presents information about BPMN Activities in rows and Resource Roles in columns. Each arrow in the matrix shows if a Resource Role is used by an Activity. 
You can define Resource Roles usage for all BPMN Activities by assigning or removing the Resource Roles to or from the Activities.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### BPMN Resource Usage Matrix

**Related elements**

- BPMN Process
- Activities
- Resource
- Organization Unit
- Role
- Person
- Information system
- BPMN Matrices

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']
- Dependency Matrix

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

1488554043

BPMN tables allow the quick creation of BPMN elements as well as easy review and modification of their properties. You can review a group of elements as one set in the same place by using these tables.

Types of BPMN tables in Cameo Business Modeler are as follows:
